tracks.title: Core Values
tracks.description: Discover the spirit of FIRST LEGO League

tracks.title: Robot Game
tracks.description: Program your robot to complete missions

tracks.title: Tournament Day
tracks.description: Be ready for judges and the event

cv-1-1.title: Welcome to FIRST LEGO League!
cv-1-1.intro: FIRST LEGO League (FLL) is a global program where kids age 9-14 design robots, solve real problems, and compete around the world. This year's theme is UNEARTHED — exploring archaeology!

questions.prompt: What does FLL stand for?
questions.explain: FIRST LEGO League — FIRST means 'For Inspiration and Recognition of Science and Technology'.

questions.prompt: What is the 2025-26 season theme?
questions.explain: UNEARTHED — all about archaeology and discovering the past!

questions.prompt: What ages can compete in FLL Challenge?
questions.explain: FLL Challenge is for ages 9-14. There's also FLL Explore (6-9) and FLL Discover (4-6).

cv-1-2.title: The 4 Parts of FLL
cv-1-2.intro: An FLL team is judged on FOUR things equally — each worth 25%. It's not just about the robot!

questions.prompt: Which is NOT one of the 4 judged parts of FLL?
questions.explain: The 4 parts are: Robot Game, Robot Design, Innovation Project, and Core Values. No costume contest!

questions.prompt: How much of your total score comes from the Robot Game?
questions.explain: Each of the 4 parts is worth exactly 25%. Many kids think it's all about the robot — it's not!

cv-2-1.title: Discovery & Innovation
cv-2-1.intro: FIRST has 6 Core Values. The first two are Discovery (exploring new skills) and Innovation (using creativity to solve problems).

questions.prompt: Which Core Value matches 'We explore new skills and ideas'?
questions.explain: Discovery is all about exploring and learning — perfect for archaeologists!

questions.prompt: Innovation means...
options: Using creativity and persistence to solve problems
questions.explain: Innovation = creativity + persistence. Even when something fails, you keep trying new ideas.

cv-2-2.title: Impact & Inclusion
cv-2-2.intro: Impact means using what you learn to improve the world. Inclusion means respecting each other and embracing differences.

questions.prompt: Your innovation project should aim for...
questions.explain: Impact is the goal — your project should solve a real problem that matters.

questions.prompt: Inclusion in FLL means...
options: Only kids who already know coding
questions.explain: FLL is for everyone. Different perspectives make stronger teams.

cv-2-3.title: Teamwork & Fun
cv-2-3.intro: The last two Core Values: Teamwork (we are stronger together) and Fun (we celebrate what we do)!

questions.prompt: Why is teamwork the most important Core Value in FLL?
options: One person can't do all 4 parts well alone
questions.explain: FLL has 4 parts — robot game, robot design, project, presentation. No single kid can master all four. Teamwork is essential.

cv-3-1.title: Gracious Professionalism
cv-3-1.intro: Gracious Professionalism™ is a FIRST term. It means doing high-quality work while respecting others. Coopertition™ means helping competitors even as you compete with them.

questions.prompt: A team next to you is having robot trouble. Gracious Professionalism says you should...
options: Offer to help if you can
questions.explain: Coopertition! Even competitors help each other in FLL. Learning matters more than winning.

questions.prompt: Gracious Professionalism is about...
options: Being polite while doing great work
questions.explain: It's a way of working: high-quality work + respect for others. You can disagree — just do it gracefully.

ed-1-1.title: The Engineering Design Process
ed-1-1.intro: Engineers don't just build things — they follow a process: Identify → Design → Create → Iterate → Communicate. Then repeat!

questions.prompt: What's the FIRST step of the engineering design process?
questions.explain: You can't solve a problem until you understand it. Identify always comes first.

questions.prompt: Why is 'Iterate' a step on its own?
options: Because nothing works the first time
questions.explain: Real engineering is mostly iteration. Your robot's first run will not score perfectly. Test, fix, test, fix.

ed-1-2.title: Identify → Design
ed-1-2.intro: Identify = pick the problem. Design = sketch a plan BEFORE building. Many teams skip Design and go straight to building — and waste hours.

questions.prompt: Your team wants to score the 'Excavate' mission. What should you do FIRST?
options: Read the rulebook + sketch a plan
questions.explain: Read + sketch first. 10 minutes of planning saves 2 hours of rebuilding.

ed-2-1.title: Iteration: Fail Fast
ed-2-1.intro: Top FLL teams test their robot 50+ times per mission. Every run teaches you something. Don't be sad about a failed run — be excited about the data!

questions.prompt: Your robot turns 92° when you tell it to turn 90°. What's the BEST next step?
options: Tell it to turn 88° instead — and test
questions.explain: Adjust by the error, then test. This is iteration. Real engineers do this all day.

questions.prompt: What's the value of a failed test run?
options: It tells you what to fix
questions.explain: Every failure is information. The teams who test the most usually win — because they learn the most.

rg-1-1.title: Move Straight
rg-1-1.intro: Your SPIKE Prime robot needs to move forward. The most basic block: 'Move'. But getting it to drive STRAIGHT is harder than it looks!

questions.prompt: Why might a robot drift left when told to drive straight?
options: Wheels are different sizes, friction is uneven, or motors are slightly different
questions.explain: Real reason: small physical differences between left/right side. Solution → use a gyro sensor for straight driving.

questions.prompt: To drive straight reliably, the BEST tool is...
options: Use the gyro sensor with feedback
questions.explain: Gyro-based driving reads the robot's heading and corrects the motors in real time. Top teams always use it.

rg-1-2.title: Distance & Time
rg-1-2.intro: Two ways to tell a robot 'how far': by time (drive for 2 seconds) or by rotation (turn the wheel 5 times). One is much more reliable!

questions.prompt: Which is more accurate: 'drive for 2 seconds' or 'drive 50 cm'?
questions.explain: Distance based on wheel rotation is much more accurate. Battery level affects time-based moves.

questions.prompt: A low battery makes time-based driving fail because...
options: Robot moves slower per second when battery is low
questions.explain: Lower voltage = slower motors = less distance per second. Check `hub.battery.voltage()` before competing!

rg-1-3.title: Acceleration & Stopping
rg-1-3.intro: Pro tip: robots that ramp up speed gradually drive straighter. Robots that brake suddenly tip forward. Smooth = accurate.

questions.prompt: A robot that suddenly brakes at full speed will...
questions.explain: Inertia tips the robot. Use 'coast' or gradual deceleration for accuracy.

rg-2-1.title: Turning with the Gyro
rg-2-1.intro: Turning by time or rotation is unreliable. Turning by GYRO ANGLE is the secret. Top teams turn within ±1° this way.

questions.prompt: To turn 90° accurately, the best method is...
questions.explain: Gyro feedback. Battery level, friction, surface — none of those affect a gyro-based turn.

questions.prompt: Before any gyro turn, you should...
questions.explain: Reset the gyro at the start of each run with `hub.imu.reset_heading(0)`. Otherwise drift accumulates.

rg-2-2.title: Pivot vs Spin Turns
rg-2-2.intro: Pivot turn = one wheel stops, other moves. Spin turn = wheels go opposite directions. They use different amounts of space!

questions.prompt: A SPIN turn (both wheels opposite) needs space because...
options: The robot rotates around its center, sweeping wider
questions.explain: The robot's corners sweep wide. In tight spots near mission models, use a pivot turn instead.

rg-3-1.title: The Color Sensor
rg-3-1.intro: FLL mats have black lines you can use to ALIGN the robot. The color sensor reads light intensity and detects edges.

questions.prompt: What's the BEST way to use lines on the FLL mat?
options: Use the color sensor to align/stop on them
questions.explain: Black lines are alignment helpers. Drive until sensor sees black → robot is now precisely positioned.

questions.prompt: Color sensors work best when...
questions.explain: Close to the mat. Otherwise ambient light interferes.

rg-3-2.title: Line Following
rg-3-2.intro: Pro skill: have the robot follow a black line. The sensor reads brightness; the robot steers toward whichever side is darker. Looks like magic.

questions.prompt: A line-following robot steers based on...
options: How dark vs bright the sensor reads (proportional control)
questions.explain: Proportional control. Sensor reads brightness → calculate error → steer proportionally. This is real robotics!

rg-4-1.title: Mission Strategy
rg-4-1.intro: You can attempt missions in ANY ORDER. Smart teams plan a route through the mat that touches multiple missions per launch.

questions.prompt: It's better to do 3 missions in one run than 1 mission per run because...
options: Setup time is limited (2.5 minutes total)
questions.explain: You only have 2.5 minutes! Each launch back to home base costs precious seconds. Combine missions when possible.

ip-1-1.title: Writing a Problem Statement
ip-1-1.intro: The Innovation Project starts with a clear problem. A good problem statement says: WHO has the problem, WHAT the problem is, and WHY it matters.

questions.prompt: Which is a good problem statement for UNEARTHED?
options: Archaeologists damage fragile artifacts when brushing soil — we need a gentler tool to preserve them for museums
We want to make a robot
questions.explain: Specific problem + who it affects + why it matters. The judges love specificity.

ip-2-1.title: Research: What Already Exists?
ip-2-1.intro: Before inventing, find out what's already been tried. This is RESEARCH. Sources: scientific papers, news, interviews with archaeologists.

questions.prompt: Why must your team research existing solutions FIRST?
options: So you don't reinvent something that already exists, and you build on real knowledge
questions.explain: Innovation = building on what exists. Pure invention from zero is rare. Stand on giants' shoulders.

jd-1-1.title: Talking to Judges
jd-1-1.intro: On event day, you'll present to judges 3 times (Robot Design, Innovation Project, Core Values). They're not scary — they want to hear YOUR story.

questions.prompt: Best way to handle a judge's question you don't know?
options: Honestly say 'we don't know yet, but we'd find out by...'
questions.explain: Honesty + a plan. Judges respect 'I don't know but here's how we'd find out' way more than a guess.

questions.prompt: Who should speak during your team's presentation?
options: Everyone — judges want to see all team members involved
questions.explain: Every team member should have a role. Judges note when only 1-2 kids do all the talking.
